name: fs
context: Read, write, watch, and manage files and directories using the Node.js fs, fs/promises, and path modules
info: The Node.js fs module provides a complete filesystem API with three calling conventions: the fs/promises interface for async/await, the callback-based fs interface for event-driven code, and the fs.*Sync interface for blocking calls in CLI scripts and startup. readFile and writeFile handle whole-file I/O, while createReadStream and createWriteStream provide backpressure-aware streaming for large files. The path module is inseparable from fs work — join, resolve, dirname, basename, and extname construct cross-platform paths without manual separator concatenation. File descriptors from open() enable low-level positioned reads and writes. stat, lstat, and access inspect metadata and permissions. watch and watchFile observe changes, though production use cases typically rely on chokidar for cross-platform consistency. mkdtemp creates unique temporary directories under os.tmpdir(). Agents use this module constantly for code generation output, configuration management, log file handling, recursive directory traversal, and build artifact manipulation.
triggers: check if a file exists in node
get file size or modification time
create a temporary file or directory

sections.title: Reading Files
sections.body: Use `readFile(path, 'utf-8')` to read a file as a string, or omit the encoding to get a Buffer. `readFileSync` blocks the event loop and should only be used at startup or in CLI scripts. For large files (over 100MB), use `createReadStream` which returns a Readable stream with automatic backpressure. Pass `{ encoding: 'utf-8' }` to the stream for text, or leave it for binary chunks. Use `for await (const chunk of stream)` to consume an async readable stream. The `{ highWaterMark }` option controls stream buffer size.

sections.title: Writing Files
sections.body: Use `writeFile(path, data)` to create or overwrite a file. Always create parent directories first with `mkdir(dirname(path), { recursive: true })`. For append operations, use `appendFile`. For atomic writes (preventing corruption on crash), write to a temporary file then `rename` it to the target path — rename is atomic on the same filesystem. Use `createWriteStream` for large outputs or when data arrives incrementally. The `{ flag: 'wx' }` option makes writeFile fail if the file already exists, useful for lock files. Handle backpressure with the `drain` event on writable streams.

sections.title: Directory Operations
sections.body: Use `mkdir(path, { recursive: true })` to create deeply nested directories without checking each level. `readdir` returns file names by default; pass `{ withFileTypes: true }` to get Dirent objects with `.isFile()`, `.isDirectory()`, and `.isSymbolicLink()` methods. Node 20+ supports `{ recursive: true }` for deep listing. `rm(path, { recursive: true, force: true })` removes a directory and all contents. `cp(src, dest, { recursive: true })` copies directory trees. `rename` moves files but fails across filesystem boundaries — use cp + rm instead.

sections.title: Watching for Changes
sections.body: The `fs.watch` API returns an async iterable of change events with `eventType` ('rename' or 'change') and `filename`. Pass `{ recursive: true }` for deep watching (macOS and Windows natively, Linux via polling). Caveats: events may fire multiple times per change, filenames may be null on some platforms, and behavior is not fully consistent across OSes. For production file watching, use the `chokidar` package which normalizes cross-platform behavior, provides glob pattern matching, ready events, and debouncing. `fs.watchFile` uses polling and is more reliable but slower.

sections.title: File Metadata and Permissions
sections.body: Use `stat` to get file size, modification time, and type checks via `isFile()`, `isDirectory()`, `isSymbolicLink()`. `stat` follows symlinks; use `lstat` to inspect the symlink itself. The `access` function checks readability (`constants.R_OK`), writability (`constants.W_OK`), and executability (`constants.X_OK`). Use `try { await access(path) } catch {}` as the idiomatic file-exists check — `fs.exists` is deprecated. The `mode` field from stat contains Unix permission bits. `utimes` sets access and modification times.

sections.title: Path Module
sections.body: Always use `path.join()` instead of string concatenation with '/' — join normalizes separators for the current OS. `path.resolve()` converts relative paths to absolute using the current working directory. `dirname`, `basename`, and `extname` extract path components. In ESM modules, `__dirname` is not available — use `dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url))` to reconstruct it. `path.parse()` splits a path into root, dir, base, ext, and name. `path.relative()` computes the relative path between two locations. On Windows, use `path.win32` or `path.posix` for explicit platform-specific behavior.
